questions.question_text: A property is located within a Public Improvement District (PID). The owner's tax burden includes:
questions.correct_answer: Regular property taxes plus PID assessments
distractors: Only PID assessments replacing property taxes
Regular property taxes with no additional cost
A one-time PID fee at purchase only
questions.explanation: PID assessments are in addition to regular property taxes. Property owners in PIDs pay both their normal property taxes and the special PID assessments for infrastructure improvements.

questions.question_text: A seller is listing a property in a PID but does not mention the PID assessments to the buyer. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A failure to make required PID disclosures
distractors: Acceptable because PIDs are public record
The buyer's responsibility to discover
Only an issue if the assessment exceeds $500
questions.explanation: Sellers must disclose PID assessments to buyers before closing. Failing to disclose ongoing special assessments is a violation of NM disclosure requirements.

questions.question_text: When a property within a PID is sold, the PID assessment obligation:
questions.correct_answer: Transfers to the new owner with the property
distractors: Is paid off by the seller at closing
Terminates upon sale of the property
Becomes optional for the new owner
questions.explanation: PID assessments run with the land, meaning they transfer to new owners when the property is sold. Buyers need to understand this ongoing financial obligation.

questions.question_text: PID assessments in NM are used to fund:
distractors: School district operating budgets
Statewide road maintenance projects
Individual property improvements only
questions.explanation: Public Improvement Districts levy special assessments to fund infrastructure improvements such as roads, utilities, parks, and drainage systems within the district boundaries.

questions.question_text: A buyer discovers after closing that the property has $3,000 in annual PID assessments that were never disclosed. The buyer may have a claim for:
questions.correct_answer: Failure to disclose a material financial burden
distractors: Property tax fraud committed by the county
Breach of the homeowner association bylaws
A zoning violation committed by the developer
questions.explanation: Undisclosed PID assessments represent a failure to disclose a significant financial obligation. The buyer may have recourse against the seller and potentially the listing broker.

questions.question_text: When selling a property in an HOA community, the seller must provide the buyer with:
distractors: Only the monthly dues amount
Only a verbal description of the HOA rules
Only the HOA president's contact information
questions.explanation: NM requires sellers to provide HOA governing documents including CC&Rs (Covenants, Conditions & Restrictions), bylaws, and financial statements to prospective buyers.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks about pending special assessments in the HOA. The seller must disclose:
distractors: Only assessments already approved and billed
Nothing because the HOA manages assessments
Only assessments exceeding $1,000
questions.explanation: Buyers must be informed of HOA dues, regular assessments, and any pending special assessments. Sellers must disclose known pending assessments even if not yet officially billed.

questions.question_text: The NM Homeowner Association Act provides:
questions.correct_answer: Consumer protections for homeowners in HOAs
distractors: Tax exemptions for HOA-managed properties
Licensing requirements for HOA board members
Mandatory state approval for HOA formation
questions.explanation: The NM Homeowner Association Act provides consumer protections for homeowners living in HOA-governed communities, including disclosure requirements and governance standards.

questions.question_text: HOA restrictions and obligations apply to a property. When the property is sold:
questions.correct_answer: The restrictions transfer with the property
distractors: The new owner can opt out of the HOA
The restrictions expire at the sale
The HOA must re-approve the new owner
questions.explanation: HOA restrictions and obligations run with the land and transfer to new owners. Buyers cannot opt out of an existing HOA — the CC&Rs are binding on all property owners in the community.

questions.question_text: A listing broker in an HOA community should ensure the buyer receives which document before making an offer?
questions.correct_answer: The HOA financial statements and bylaws
distractors: A letter of approval from the HOA president
The original developer's construction plans
A survey of all common area boundaries
questions.explanation: Sellers must provide HOA governing documents including financial statements and bylaws. This allows buyers to understand the financial health of the HOA and the community rules before committing.
